An Account Lock Policy is a security measure used to protect accounts from unauthorized access or brute -
force attacks. It specifies the conditions under which an account will be locked after a set number of failed 
login attempts. Here’s a breakdown of typical elements that are p art of an Account Lock policy:

metadata.section: An Account Lock Policy is a security measure used to protect accounts from unaut

page_content:
1. Failed Login Attempts 
• Defines the number of unsuccessful login attempts allowed before locking the account. For example, 
an account may be locked after 5 failed attempts.

2. Lockout Duration 
• Specifies how long the account will be locked. It could be a fixed period (e.g., 30 minutes ) or until an 
administrator manually unlocks it.

3. Re-locking Conditions 
• Describes whether the failed login attempts will be reset after a certain period (e.g., 15 minutes ) or if 
the counter continues to accumulate until an administrator intervenes.

4. Account Unlocking Procedure 
• Outlines how to unlock the account, such as via a self -service password reset, administrator 
intervention, or using a recovery email or phone number.

5. Notification 
• A system or administrator may send notifications to users or administrators when an account is locked 
or when a threshold of failed login attempts is approaching.

6. Exclusion List (optional) 
• Certain high -level or trusted users might be excluded from the lockout policy to prevent them from 
being locked out due to failed login attempts (though this is rare due to security risks).

7. Security Considerations 
• Some policies might include additional layers, such as implementing CAPTCHA after a certain number 
of failed attempts or requiring multi -factor authentication (MFA) after a lockout event. 
This policy is commonly implemented in web applications, email systems, and enterprise networks to reduce 
the risk of unauthorized access from malicious actors.

1. Purpose 
This policy establishes guidelines and procedures for the disposal of assets to ensure accountability , 
compliance , and efficiency in managing the organization’s resources.

2. Scope 
This policy applies to all physical and intangible assets owned by the organization, including but not 
limited to: 
• Equipment 
• Furniture 
• IT hardware 
• Vehicles 
• Intellectual property

3. Asset Disposal Principles 
• Assets should only be disposed of when they are no longer required, obsolete, damaged 
beyond repair, or deemed uneconomical to maintain. 
• Disposal must be conducted in a transparent and accountable manner. 
• Environmental considerations should be considered to promote sustainability. 
• Compliance with legal , regulatory , and financial requirements must be ensured.

4. Disposal Methods 
Assets may be disposed of using one or more of the following methods: 
• Transfer : Transferring assets to other departments within the organization if still usable. 
• Sales : Selling assets through public auction, tender, or direct sale at fair market value. 
• Donation : Donating assets to charitable organizations or educational institutions where 
appropriate. 
• Recycling or Scrapping : Environmentally responsible recycling or scrapping of non -usable 
assets. 
• Trade -in: Exchanging old assets for new ones with a supplier.

5. Authorization and Approval 
• The disposal of assets must be approved by the designated authority based on asset value 
thresholds. 
• A disposal request form must be submitted with justification for disposal and valuation 
details. 
• High -value assets require board or senior management approval .

6. Record Keeping 
• All asset disposals must be documented, including: 
o Asset descriptions Asset Disposal Policy

o Disposal methods 
o Approvals 
o Financial records 
• Records must be maintained for audit and compliance purposes.

7. Compliance and Accountability 
• All employees involved in asset disposal must adhere to this policy. 
• Non -compliance may result in disciplinary action .

8. Review and Amendments 
This policy shall be reviewed periodically to ensure continued relevance and compliance with 
regulatory requirements.

1. Objective 
To design a framework for activation of a Business Continuity Plan (BCP) in a situation of disaster. A 
disaster is defined as “any unplanned event that results in the inability of the business to support 
operations in whole or in part”. A business is vulne rable to four different types of disaster: 
• Natural disasters 
• Willful damage 
• Accidental damage 
• Global Pandemic 
To minimize the damage to business by such an occurrence, it is necessary to have a recovery plan, 
which addresses the worst -case scenario. Hence objective is to design a framework to enable Work 
from Home for Contact Centre agents to support BCP . 
In scope: 
 
Banking operations across all businesses operating from the Partner sites for IBVL Processes.

2. Communication and Enablement 
IT Management Group 
Partner to form a body of IT Management Group. The IT Management Group is responsible for 
controlling all technical aspects of emergency response. The group will be led by the Authorized 
Person. Members of this group (and only these designated members) hav e the authority to: 
• Order facility shutdown 
• Initiate evacuation of employees and customers 
• Declare that an incident has Started / Ended 
• Communicate with respective stakeholders 
• Activate & execute BCP plan

3. Context of Operational Risks 
In the backdrop of growing complexity of financial products and the increased leveraging of 
technology and its heightened sophistication, operational risks have assumed critical importance in 
recent times. The treatment of operational risks as a distinct r isk category along with credit and 
market risks in the Basel II framework is a manifestation of the vital role played by operational risks 
in impacting risk profile of an IBVL. Operational risks can also have a systemic connotation in the event 
of contagio n through channels like the payment system and undermine public confidence in the 
banking system.

4. Importance of BCP 
Business Continuity Planning is a key pre -requisite for minimizing the adverse effects of one of the 
important areas of operational risk – business disruption and system failures. A recent study Business Conti nuity Plan

conducted by RBI revealed that some BFSI were still in the process of framing a Business Continuity 
Plan (BCP). It is imperative that IBVL will have BCPs in place to be in readiness to tackle serious 
business disruptions.

metadata.section: conducted by RBI revealed that some BFSI were still in the process of framing a

page_content:
5. Governance Responsibility 
The responsibility in respect of BCP rests with the Board of Directors and the top management of 
IBVL. The Board will provide top management clear guidance and direction in relation to BCP . The 
Board fulfils its responsibilities by: 
• Approving policy on BCP 
• Prioritizing critical business functions 
• Allocating sufficient resources 
• Reviewing BCP test results 
• Ensuring maintenance and periodic updating of BCP 
The BCP requirements enunciated in this circular will be considered as a minimum. The onus is on the 
Board and the top management for generating detailed components of BCP in the light of individual 
bank's activities, systems and processes. The top managem ent is responsible for executing such a BCP 
if contingency arises. The top management will annually review the adequacy of the institution's 
business recovery, contingency plans and the test results and put the same to the Board. The top 
management will al so evaluate the adequacy of contingency planning and their periodic testing by 
service providers whenever critical operations are outsourced.

6. Business Interdependencies 
Changing business processes (internally to the institution and externally among interdependent 
financial service providers) and new threat scenarios require maintenance of viable BCPs. An effective 
BCP will take into account: 
• The potential for wide disasters that impact an entire region and for the resulting loss or 
inaccessibility of staff 
• Interdependencies, both market -based and geographic, among financial system participants 
as well as infrastructure service providers

7. Timeline and Testing 
It is, therefore, advised that IBVL will put in place a BCP including a robust information risk 
management system, if not already implemented, within a fixed time frame. They will implement 
such BCP and thoroughly test it to verify its full capability agai nst the changing scenario and 
assumptions at frequent intervals, as per the policy. The plan will also be subjected to review annually.

8. BCP Methodology Will Include: 
• Identification of critical businesses, owned and shared resources with supporting functions 
(the BCP template shall include IT Continuity Plan template) 
• Structured risk assessment based on comprehensive Business Impact Analysis (BIA) 
• Formulating Recovery Time Objectives (RTO) based on BIA, periodically fine -tuned by 
benchmarking against industry best practices

• Critical and tough assumptions in terms of disaster so that the framework would be exhaustive 
enough to address the most stressful situations 
• Identification of the Recovery Point Objective (RPO) for data loss for each of the critical 
systems and strategy to deal with such data loss 
• Alternate procedures during the time systems are not available 
• Clearly documented and tested processes for shifting to secondary/back -up systems and sites 
• Risk management by implementing IS design and architecture to attain the bank’s agreed RTOs 
and RPOs 
Also includes: 
• Minimizing immediate damage and losses 
• Restoring critical business functions, including customer -facing systems and payment & 
settlement systems like cash disbursements, ATMs, Internet banking, call centers, etc. 
• Establishing management succession and emergency powers 
• Addressing HR issues and training aspects 
• Providing for the safety and wellbeing of people in the branch or at the location at the time of 
the disaster 
• Use of external resources/support 
• Having specific contingency plans for each outsourcing arrangement based on the degree of 
materiality of the outsourced activity to the bank's business 
• Ensuring service providers for critical operations have BCPs in place and also periodically test 
the same 
• Compatibility and co -ordination of contingency plans at both the bank and its service 
providers 
• Action plans, practical manuals and testing procedures 
• Independent audit and review of the BCP and test results 
• Periodic updating to absorb changes in the institution or its service providers

metadata.section: • Critical and tough assumptions in terms of disaster so that the framework woul

page_content:
9. Plan Development and Dissemination 
The BCP will consider the project management procedures, change management process, data center 
process, backup and recovery process. Based on a sound methodology, a development plan for the 
DRS / BCP will be initiated. The plan needs to be continuously ev aluated and revised whenever the 
bank forays into new business tools and areas, either as part of a reengineering process or for 
introducing new products and services. 
The relevant portion of the BCP adopted will also be disseminated to all concerned, including the 
customers, so that the awareness would enable them to react positively and in consonance with the 
BCP . The part of the plan kept in the public domain will nor mally be confined to information relating 
to the general readiness of the IBVL in this regard without any detailed specifics.

10. Cost Implication 
BCP involves cost implications. While IBVL will consider cost -effective strategies for BCP , the strategies 
considered will provide an adequate level of comfort and assurance in tackling serious disruptions.

Moreover, the mitigating solution will be commensurate with the nature and complexity of their 
business operations.

page_content:
11. Insurance as a Risk Mitigation Strategy 
BVPL will also consider insurance as a risk mitigation strategy for externalizing risks to third parties to 
reduce financial exposure in the event of disruption. However, diligence needs to be exercised 
regarding the nature of insurance and the certainty o f payments.

4. Policy Requirements 
• Backup of all business data, related application systems and other business critical 
applications, wherein the frequency of backup operations and the procedures for recovery 
and restoration meets the need of the availability of data to business in case of a disaster or 
loss of data due to errors and omissions either advertent or inadvertent. 
• Archiving electronic data shall be in accordance with the business, legal and regulatory 
requirements. Archiving shall be done to ensure that stakeholders have easy access on a need -
to-know basis of data / information. 
• Data backup strategy and data recovery procedures shall be implemented to ensure that 
critical business data is not lost under any circumstances and is always available. 
• Data Purging of all electronic data, images and any other information pertaining to client shall 
be carried out according to guidelines laid down by client as per defined periodicity of data. 
• Access Control: Access to the common drive will be granted based on the principle of least 
privilege, with access rights determined by the employee's role and responsibilities approved 
by Ops Head & IT SMT. 
• Data Security : Employees are responsible for maintaining the confidentiality and integrity of 
data stored on the common drive. Data Backup, Retention and Purging Policy

• All sensitive or confidential information must be appropriately labeled, and access restricted 
to authorized personnel only. 
• Deletion of data without proper approval is prohibited. Also, you will update, delete data only 
which is yours from common drive, if found that you are deleting data of someone else, ZTP 
will be applied. 
• File Organization: Employees are required to organize files in a logical and consistent manner 
to facilitate easy retrieval and minimize clutter. 
• Files should be named descriptively and include relevant dates or version numbers if 
applicable. 
• Usage Guidelines: The common drive is intended for storing and sharing work -related 
documents only. Personal files or non -work -related content are not permitted. 
• Employees must adhere to all relevant laws, regulations, and company policies when 
accessing or storing data on the common drive. 
• Data which is needed in the long run (older than 90 days & unwanted) should not be stored 
on common drive and should be deleted or with approval from SLA should be transferred to 
respective client. 
• Bandwidth Management: Editing or working on files simultaneously with multiple users in 
one file via the common drive is discouraged to prevent excessive strain on internet 
bandwidth. 
• Employees should copy and paste files to their local drive for editing, then upload the revised 
file back to the common drive upon completion. 
• Data Backup: It is the responsibility of individual employees to ensure that important files are 
backed up (to client location or to client repository) regularly to prevent data loss. 
• The IT department will implement regular backups of the common drive, but employees 
should not rely solely on this backup for critical files, nor will this backup be responsible for 
providing deleted files or lost files. 
• Prohibited Actions: Uploading or storing malicious software, illegal content, or any material 
that violates company policies or applicable laws is strictly prohibited. 
• Any attempts to bypass security measures or access unauthorized areas of the common drive 
will result in disciplinary action.

page_content:
1. Purpose 
The purpose of this document is to outline the baseline hardening procedures for IT equipment used 
within the organization to mitigate security risks and ensure compliance with industry standards.

2. Scope 
This document applies to all IT equipment including but not limited to: 
• Servers (physical and virtual) 
• Desktops and laptops 
• Network devices (routers, switches, firewalls) 
• Mobile devices 
• Storage devices 
• Printers and IoT devices

3. General Hardening Principles 
• Least Privilege: Users and services should run with the minimum level of access necessary. 
• Patch Management: Regular updates and patches must be applied to all systems. 
• Disable Unused Services: Turn off all unnecessary services, ports, and features. 
• Change Default Credentials: Default usernames and passwords must be changed upon setup. 
• Logging and Monitoring: Enable logging and implement a centralized monitoring system.

4. Hardening Procedures by Equipment Type 
4.1 Servers 
• Install only required roles and features. 
• Disable unused ports and services. 
• Implement anti -malware and endpoint detection. 
• Configure host -based firewalls. 
• Apply OS hardening scripts or tools (e.g., CIS Benchmarks). 
• Enforce secure SSH/RDP configurations. 
4.2 Workstations (Laptops/Desktops) 
• Apply full disk encryption (e.g., BitLocker). 
• Enforce strong password policies. 
• Disable USB access where not needed. 
• Install antivirus/endpoint protection software. 
• Restrict local admin access. 
• Ensure auto -lock screensaver policies are active. 
4.3 Network Equipment 
• Change default SNMP strings and credentials. 
• Disable unused interfaces. 
• Implement port security and MAC address filtering. 
• Use secure management protocols (e.g., SSH, HTTPS). Data Privacy Policy

• Backup configurations regularly. 
• Apply firmware updates. 
4.4 Mobile Devices 
• Enable device encryption and remote wipe capabilities. 
• Require PIN or biometric authentication. 
• Disable unnecessary apps and services. 
• Use containerization for corporate data. 
4.5 Printers and IoT Devices 
• Change default admin credentials. 
• Disable unused protocols (FTP , Telnet, etc.). 
• Segment these devices on isolated VLANs. 
• Regularly update firmware. 
• Enable logging where available.

5. Compliance and Auditing 
• Regular vulnerability assessments and penetration tests must be conducted. 
• Maintain audit logs of configuration changes. 
• Review hardening compliance quarterly. 
• Use automated tools (e.g., Nessus, OpenVAS, SCCM, etc.) for audits.

6. Exceptions 
Any deviations from these hardening guidelines must be documented and approved by the IT Security 
Team.

1. Purpose 
This policy establishes guidelines for the use of an outbound dia ler system to ensure compliance with 
legal and regulatory requirements, protect customer rights, and maintain ethical dial ing practices.

2. Scope 
This policy applies to all employees, agents, and third -party vendors utilizing the dia ler system for 
outbound communications.

3. Regulatory Compliance 
All dial ing activities must comply with the following regulations: 
• Telephone Consumer Protection Act (TCPA) 
• Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA) 
• General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) (if applicable) 
• Do Not Call (DNC) Registry compliance 
• State and local telemarketing laws 
• Other industry -specific regulations

4. Dia ling Rules and Restrictions 
• Calls must not be made outside legally permitted hours. 
• The dia ler must suppress numbers listed on national, state, or internal Do Not Call lists. 
• Prior express written consent is required for auto -dialed or prerecorded calls to mobile 
numbers. 
• Abandonment rates must not exceed 3% of answered calls per campaign, measured over a 
30-day period. 
• The dial er must provide an opt -out mechanism for recipients to stop further calls. 
• Caller ID must display accurate company information.

5. Call Monitoring and Quality Assurance 
• All calls may be recorded and monitored for compliance and training purposes. 
• Supervisors must conduct regular audits of dia ler activities to ensure adherence to policies. 
• Agents must be trained on compliance requirements before using the dia ler.

6. Customer Consent and Opt -Out Handling 
• Maintain records of customer consent for telemarketing and automated calls. 
• Customers must be given a clear option to opt out during calls and through a documented 
process. 
• Opt-out requests must be honored within the legally mandated timeframe. 
 
 
 Dialer Policy

7. Data Security and Confidentiality 
• Customer data used in the dial er must be protected per data security policies and applicable 
regulations. 
• Only authorized personnel should have access to dial er systems. 
• Personal Identifiable Information (PII) must be handled in accordance with company data 
protection policies.

8. Violations and Disciplinary Actions 
• Non -compliance with this policy may result in disciplinary actions, including termination. 
• Regulatory violations may lead to fines, penalties, or legal actions. 
• Employees must report any suspected violations immediately to the compliance officer.

9. Review and Updates 
This policy must be reviewed annually and updated as necessary to align with regulatory changes and 
business needs.

10. Acknowledgment 
All employees and agents using the dial er must acknowledge receipt and understanding of this policy.

1. Purpose 
This policy outlines the process for offboarding employees to ensure compliance, data security, and 
smooth transitions while protecting company assets.

2. Scope 
This policy applies to all full -time, part -time, and contract employees across all locations within the 
organization.

3. Roles and Responsibilities 
• HR Department: Manages employee communication, documentation, and compliance. 
• IT Department: Ensures the timely revocation of system access and retrieval of assets. 
• Team Manager: Oversees knowledge transfer and ensures continuity of project work.

Step 1: Notification and Documentation 
• Upon receiving resignation or termination notice, HR initiates the offboarding process. 
• An official letter of resignation/termination should be documented. 
• HR creates an exit checklist to track the process.

Step 2: Access Revocation 
• IT must disable all system access, including: 
o Email accounts 
o User accounts across all internal systems and applications 
• Physical access tools such as keycards, access badges, and office keys must be retrieved. 
• Ensure removal from third -party systems, including vendor portals and code repositories.

Step 3: Asset Retrieval and Data Management 
• Employees must return company -issued assets such as laptops, phones, and peripherals. 
• IT performs a secure data wipe on returned devices. 
• Managers should ensure that project files, credentials, and documentation are transitioned to 
appropriate team members.

Step 4: Financial and Benefits Settlement 
• HR processes the employee's final payroll, including unused leave, bonuses, or 
reimbursements. 
• Benefits such as insurance, retirement plans, and memberships must be reviewed for 
termination or transfer.

Step 5: Knowledge Transfer 
• Managers must arrange a knowledge transfer session to ensure continuity of tasks. Employee Offboarding Poli cy

Overview 
A Firewall Policy is a set of rules that define how a firewall should handle network traffic. It outlines 
the conditions under which data can enter or exit a network, what kind of traffic is allowed, and what 
should be blocked. The policy is crucial for maintaining the security and integrity of the network by 
preventing unauthorized access , detecting threats , and controlling traffic flow based on the 
organization's security requirements. 
 
Key Components of a Firewall Policy

1. Access Control Rules 
• Allow or Deny rules for inbound and outbound traffic. 
• Specific rules based on source IP addresses, destination IP addresses, ports, protocols, or 
applications.

2. Traffic Direction 
• Inbound traffic : Rules that apply to data entering the network. 
• Outbound traffic : Rules for data leaving the network. 
• Internal traffic : Rules that govern communications within the internal network.

3. Service Control 
• Policies to control access to specific services or applications (e.g., HTTP , FTP , DNS, or custom 
services).

4. Network Address Translation (NAT) 
• Rules defining how internal IP addresses are mapped to public IP addresses for outgoing 
traffic.

5. Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS) 
• Some firewalls are integrated with IDS/IPS features to detect and block malicious activities.

6. Logging and Monitoring 
• A firewall policy often includes logging of allowed and denied traffic to monitor and detect 
suspicious activities. 
• Alerts and notifications can be set up to inform network administrators of potential threats.

7. VPN and Remote Access Rules 
• A firewall policy might govern the use of Virtual Private Networks (VPNs) for secure remote 
access.

8. Security Zones 
• Firewalls often segment the network into zones (e.g., internal , DMZ , and external ). Policies 
can be applied differently based on these zones.

9. Quality of Service (QoS) 
• Sometimes firewalls are configured to prioritize certain types of traffic (e.g., voice or video) 
over others to ensure optimal performance. 
 
Types of Firewall Policies
1. Default Deny Policy (Least Privilege) Firewall Policy

metadata.section: Default Deny Policy (Least Privilege) Firewall Policy

page_content:
• By default, all traffic is denied unless explicitly allowed by a rule. This is considered a secure 
and restrictive policy.

metadata.section: • By default, all traffic is denied unless explicitly allowed by a rule. This is

page_content:
2. Default Allow Policy 
• By default, all traffic is allowed unless explicitly blocked. This policy can be riskier and is often 
used in less sensitive environments .

3. Stateful vs. Stateless Policies 
• Stateful firewalls track the state of active connections and apply policies accordingly. 
• Stateless firewalls examine each packet independently and apply rules without tracking 
connections.

4. Role -Based Policies 
• Firewalls can be configured to enforce different access levels for different users, devices, or 
applications based on roles.

5. Application Layer Firewall Policies 
• Policies that control access based on specific applications or services (e.g., blocking certain 
websites or web applications). 
 
Best Practices for Creating a Firewall Policy

1. Least Privilege 
• Start by blocking all traffic and then allow only the traffic necessary for business functions.

2. Regular Audits 
• Regularly review and update firewall policies to ensure they are effective against new threats .

3. Segmentation 
• Create separate rules for different segments of the network (e.g., internal network , DMZ , and 
external network ).

4. Logging and Monitoring 
• Implement thorough logging and review logs regularly to detect unusual activity.

5. Use Strong Authentication 
• Apply strong authentication mechanisms , particularly for remote access policies.

6. Test Policies Before Deployment 
• Always test policies in a controlled environment before deploying them into production.

7. Documenting Policies 
• Ensure that all firewall rules and configurations are documented and well understood by the 
security team. 
 
Conclusion 
A well-defined firewall policy plays a critical role in an organization's overall cybersecurity strategy , 
ensuring that network traffic is securely managed while allowing legitimate communication to flow.

5. Disciplinary Policy 
Initial Investigation 
• When an information security breach is suspected or reported, the incident response team 
initiates an initial investigation to determine the nature and scope of the breach. 
Assessment of Severity 
• The severity of the breach is assessed based on the impact on confidentiality, integrity, and 
availability of information assets. 
• Breaches are categorized into different levels of severity (e.g., low, medium, high) based on 
predefined criteria. 
Identification of Responsible Party 
• The individuals or teams responsible for the breach are identified through the investigation 
process. 
• The investigation seeks to establish whether the breach was intentional or unintentional, and 
whether it was caused by negligence or malicious intent. 
Documentation 
• Detailed documentation of the incident, including findings from the investigation, is prepared. 
• Documentation includes evidence gathered, actions taken, and recommendations for 
preventing similar breaches in the future. 
Notification 
• The individuals responsible are notified of the findings of the investigation and the disciplinary 
process that will follow. 
Disciplinary Action 
• Disciplinary action is taken in accordance with the severity of the breach and the 
organization's policies.

• Possible disciplinary actions may include: 
o Verbal or written warnings for minor breaches or first -time offenses 
o Suspension from work for serious breaches pending further investigation 
o Termination of employment for repeated or severe breaches, or breaches involving 
malicious intent 
Appeals Process 
• Employees who are subject to disciplinary action have the right to appeal against the decision. 
• An appeals process is in place to ensure fairness and transparency. 
Communication 
• The outcome of the disciplinary action is communicated to all relevant stakeholders, including 
the incident response team, management, and HR. 
Training and Awareness 
• Training and awareness programs are conducted to educate employees about the importance 
of information security and the consequences of breaching security policies. 
Review and Continuous Improvement 
• The incident management policy and disciplinary process are periodically reviewed and 
updated to reflect changes in technology, regulations, and organizational requirements. 
• Lessons learned from security breaches are used to improve security controls and prevent 
future incidents.

6. Policy Review 
• The Incident Management Policy will be reviewed every 12 months by specified IT Admin 
personnel for incorporation of new guidelines or modifications to existing guidelines or 
processes and changes updated in Document Control Sheet .

2. Background 
Information technology (IT) resources require on -going investment of professional and financial 
resources to perform acceptably. The total expense to acquire and operate IT equipment typically 
involves much more than the initial purchase price of hardware or software. The collective costs of 
running a computer system over its lifespan can include network connections, license renewals, 
software upgrades, and IT support .

3. Hardware and Software Purchase Guidelines 
i. IBVL is responsible for maintaining the network, servers, workstations, and peripherals to ensure 
that quality is maintained at reasonable cost. As such, procurement of all IT resources and services is 
to be centrally managed IBVL. Manages the purchase of IT related items in collaboration with the 
purchasing department, which has the final responsibility and authority for vendor selection and all 
purchasing decisions. 
ii. A centralized IT procurement procedure benefits Administration by providing: 
• Brand and model standardization where appropriate 
• Office -wise inventory of hardware and software to facilitate effective planning 
• Pricing advantages obtained through volume purchasing and working with preferred vendors 
• License compliance for software purchases 
• Hardware and software with a known “support state” at the time of purchase

4. IT Procurement Process 
When a department decides to purchase IT equipment, software, and/or consumables: 
a. Contact IBVL to place the initial request. 
b. User Services will work with the department to verify and determine the equipment which best 
meets their requirements. 
c. Once the appropriate equipment has been chosen, the customer will provide User Services with a 
valid index to charge the purchase. 
d. Upon approval, IBVL will work with the Purchasing Department to procure the item. 
e. Upon receipt, the item(s) will be configured, delivered, installed, and tested by IBVL personnel. All 
expenditure will be allocated directly to the department when purchasing via Purchase Order. For

f. All IT equipment purchased with IT funds remains the property of the IT until the disposal and

1. Purpose 
The purpose of this document is to outline the baseline hardening procedures for IT equipment used 
within the organization to mitigate security risks and ensure compliance with industry standards .

2. Scope 
This document applies to all IT equipment including, but not limited to: 
• Servers (physical and virtual) 
• Desktops and laptops 
• Network devices (routers, switches, firewalls) 
• Mobile devices 
• Storage devices 
• Printers and IoT devices

3. General Hardening Principles 
• Least Privilege : Users and services should run with the minimum level of access necessary. 
• Patch Management : Regular updates and patches must be applied to all systems. 
• Disable Unused Services : Turn off all unnecessary services, ports, and features . 
• Change Default Credentials : Default usernames and passwords must be changed upon setup. 
• Logging and Monitoring : Enable logging and implement a centralized monitoring system.

4. Hardening Procedures by Equipment Type 
4.1 Servers 
• Install only required roles and features. 
• Disable unused ports and services. 
• Implement anti -malware and endpoint detection. 
• Configure host -based firewalls. 
• Apply OS hardening scripts or tools (e.g., CIS Benchmarks). 
• Enforce secure SSH/RDP configurations. 
4.2 Workstations (Laptops/Desktops) 
• Apply full disk encryption. 
• Enforce strong password policies. 
• Disable USB access where not needed. 
• Install antivirus/endpoint protection software. 
• Restrict local admin access. 
• Ensure auto -lock screensaver policies are active. 
4.3 Network Equipment 
• Change default SNMP strings and credentials. 
• Disable unused interfaces. 
• Implement port security and MAC address filtering. 
• Use secure management protocols (e.g., SSH, HTTPS). IT Equipment Hardening Policy

• Backup configurations regularly. 
• Apply firmware updates. 
4.4 Mobile Devices 
• Enable device encryption and remote wipe capabilities. 
• Require PIN or biometric authentication. 
• Disable unnecessary apps and services. 
• Use containerization for corporate data. 
4.5 Printers and IoT Devices 
• Change default admin credentials. 
• Disable unused protocols (FTP , Telnet, etc.). 
• Segment these devices on isolated VLANs. 
• Regularly update firmware. 
• Enable logging where available.

5. Compliance and Auditing 
• Regular vulnerability assessments and penetration tests must be conducted. 
• Maintain audit logs of configuration changes. 
• Review hardening compliance quarterly.

Policy Statement 
• IBVL recognizes the critical importance of logging and monitoring critical user activities, 
network traffic, security events, and IT infrastructure capacity to detect and respond to 
security incidents effectively. 
• This policy establishes guidelines and procedures for defining, documenting, and approving 
logging and monitoring activities by management.

2. Policy Ownership and Approval 
• The policy for logging and monitoring of critical user activities, network traffic, security events, 
and IT infrastructure capacity shall be defined, documented, and approved by the 
management of IBVL . 
• The Information Security Team shall oversee the implementation and ensure compliance with 
this policy.

3. Log Management 
Logs shall be maintained to capture the following activities, at a minimum: 
a) All use (both authorized and unauthorized) of BFL’s accounts or credentials provided to 
service providers for use on behalf of BFL (e.g., BFL email IDs). 
b) Suspicious activities such as failed logins, start and stop of services, modification of user 
privileges, and denial of service attempts. 
c) All activities performed by administrative or privileged accounts . 
d) Inbound and outbound network traffic via the firewall.

4. Log Review 
• Logs, including network traffic, critical user activities, etc., shall be reviewed periodically , at 
least annually , to detect anomalies, security incidents, and performance issues. 
• The Information Security Team shall conduct these reviews and promptly investigate any 
identified issues.

5. Monitoring of IT Infrastructure Capacity 
• The capacity of IT infrastructure components —including storage requirements, bandwidth 
usage, and processor utilization —shall be monitored regularly to ensure availability and 
optimal performance of IT assets. 
• Any capacity constraints or performance degradation shall be addressed promptly to maintain 
service continuity.
6. Availability of Log Collection and Storage Infrastructure Logging and Monitoring Pol icy

metadata.section: Availability of Log Collection and Storage Infrastructure Logging and Monitoring

page_content:
• IBVL shall ensure that necessary processes and IT infrastructure are available for the 
collection, storage, and analysis of logs, including critical user activities, network traffic, and 
security events. 
• This includes deploying appropriate logging tools , storage systems, and analytical capabilities .

metadata.section: • IBVL shall ensure that necessary processes and IT infrastructure are available

page_content:
7. Log Retention Policy 
• A log retention policy shall be defined, specifying the duration for which logs must be retained 
based on regulatory requirements, industry standards, and operational needs. 
• Logs shall be retained securely and consistently , and their disposal shall be conducted in 
accordance with the retention policy.

8. Access Controls for Logs 
• Access to edit or delete logs shall be restricted to a limited number of users with legitimate 
need (e.g., system administrators). 
• These users shall not be involved in log review activities to maintain the integrity and 
independence of monitoring processes.

9. Compliance and Auditing 
• Compliance with this policy shall be monitored through regular audits and assessments 
conducted by the Information Security Team or designated auditors. 
• Any non-compliance or deficiencies identified shall be addressed promptly to ensure the 
effectiveness and reliability of logging and monitoring practices.

10. Training and Awareness 
• Employees involved in logging and monitoring activities shall receive training and awareness 
sessions to ensure they understand: 
o Their roles and responsibilities 
o Logging and monitoring procedures 
o The importance of maintaining accurate and secure logs for security and compliance 
purposes

11. Continuous Improvement 
• IBVL shall regularly review and update logging and monitoring processes, tools, and 
procedures to: 
o Incorporate industry best practices 
o Address emerging threats 
o Enhance the effectiveness and efficiency of logging and monitoring

12. Incident Response Integration 
• Logging and monitoring processes shall be integrated with the incident response plan to 
enable timely detection, investigation , and response to security incidents and breaches .

• Logs shall be used as critical sources of evidence during incident response and forensic 
investigations .

1. Introduction 
This policy outlines the procedures and guidelines for logical provisioning within IBVL. It establishes 
a structured workflow where access requests are initiated by the Human Resources (HR) department, 

department. The policy aims to ensure efficient and secure provisioning of logical access to systems 
and data, in alignment with organizational objectives and regulatory requirements.

2. Overview of Logical Provisioning 
Logical provisioning refers to the process of granting and managing access to digital resources, such 
as systems, applications, and data, based on predefined roles and permissions. In this workflow, HR 
serves as the initiator of access requests, while an a dministrative department acts as an intermediary 
for review and validation before forwarding requests to the IT department for implementation.

3. Policy Statement 
• Access to digital resources within the organization shall be initiated by HR through the 
designated access request process. 
• An administrative department shall be responsible for reviewing and validating access 
requests before forwarding them to the IT department. 
• The IT department shall implement approved access requests promptly and efficiently, 
following established procedures.

4. Process Overview 
• Initiation by HR: HR personnel shall initiate access requests on behalf of employees using the 
designated access request form, providing necessary details such as employee information, 
access requirements, and justification. 
• Review by Administrative Department: Access requests received from HR shall be reviewed 
and validated by the administrative department to ensure compliance with organizational 
policies and procedures. Any discrepancies or additional information required shall be 
communicated back to HR for c larification. 
• Submission to IT Department: Upon approval by the administrative department, access 
requests shall be forwarded to the IT department for implementation. IT personnel shall 
execute the provisioning of access according to the approved specifications within a 
reasonable timeframe. 
• Provisioning and Deprovisioning Procedures: IT shall ensure the provisioning of access is 
carried out securely and promptly, adhering to established access control measures. In case 
of employee role changes, transfers, or departures, IT shall promptly update access 
permissions or deactivate account s as necessary. 
• Documentation and Record Keeping: HR, administrative, and IT departments shall maintain 
accurate records of access requests, approvals, and provisioning activities for auditing and 
compliance purposes. Logical Access Ma nagement Policy

5. Roles and Responsibilities 
• HR Department: Responsible for initiating access requests on behalf of employees and 
providing necessary information to facilitate the provisioning process. 
• Administrative Department: Responsible for reviewing and validating access requests, 
ensuring compliance with organizational policies and procedures. 
• IT/Security Department: Responsible for implementing approved access requests promptly 
and securely, maintaining appropriate access controls, and updating access permissions as 
needed. 
• Employees: Responsible for adhering to organizational policies and procedures related to 
access to digital resources, providing accurate information to HR for access requests, and 
promptly reporting any access -related issues or concerns.

6. Access Request Form 
An access request form template shall be provided to HR for initiating access requests. The form shall 
include fields for employee information, access requirements, justification, and any other relevant 
details.

7. Approval Workflow 
• Initiation by HR: HR personnel shall initiate access requests on behalf of employees using the 
designated access request form, providing necessary details such as employee information, 
access requirements, and justification. 
• Review by Administrative Department: Access requests received from HR shall be reviewed 
and validated by the administrative department to ensure compliance with organizational 
policies and procedures. Any discrepancies or additional information required shall be 
communicated back to HR for c larification. 
• Submission to IT Department: Upon approval by the administrative department, access 
requests shall be forwarded to the IT department for implementation. IT personnel shall 
execute the provisioning of access according to the approved specifications within a 
reasonable timeframe. 
• Provisioning and Deprovisioning Procedures: IT shall ensure the provisioning of access is 
carried out securely and promptly, adhering to established access control measures. In case 
of employee role changes, transfers, or departures, IT shall promptly update access 
permissions or deactivate account s as necessary. 
• Documentation and Record Keeping: HR, administrative, and IT departments shall maintain 
accurate records of access requests, approvals, and provisioning activities for auditing and 
compliance purposes.

8. Security Considerations 
Access control measures shall be implemented and maintained by the IT department to ensure the 
confidentiality, integrity, and availability of organizational digital resources.

9. Training and Awareness 
HR, administrative, and IT personnel shall receive training on the logical provisioning process, 
including their respective roles and responsibilities. Additionally, employees shall be made aware of 
access -related policies and procedures through regular co mmunication and training initiatives .

10. Compliance and Auditing 
The logical provisioning process shall comply with relevant regulatory requirements and undergo 
regular audits and reviews to ensure effectiveness and adherence to established policies and 
procedures.

11. Policy Review and Revision 
This policy shall be reviewed annually to ensure its relevance, effectiveness, and alignment with 
organizational objectives. Any necessary revisions shall be made following an established review 
process.

12. Policy Acknowledgment 
All employees shall be required to acknowledge their understanding and compliance with this policy 
upon joining the organization and periodically thereafter.

13. Enforcement 
Non -compliance with this policy may result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of 
employment, in accordance with organizational policies and procedures.

1. Overview 
Acceptable use of IBVL owned mobile devices must be managed to ensure that employees, Board of 
Directors, and related constituents who use mobile devices to access IBVL’s resources for business 
do so in a safe and secure manner. 
This policy is designed to maximize the degree to which private and confidential data is protected 
from both deliberate and inadvertent exposure and/or breach.

2. Purpose 
This policy defines the standards, procedures, and restrictions for end users who have legitimate 
business requirements to access corporate data from a mobile device connected to an unmanaged 
network outside of IBVL’s direct control. 
This mobile device policy applies to, but is not limited to, any mobile device issued by IBVL that 
contains stored data owned by IBVL and all devices and accompanying media that fit the following 
device classifications: 
• Notebooks, and hybrid devices 
• Tablets 
• Mobile/cellular phones including smartphones 
• Any IBVL owned mobile device capable of storing corporate data and connecting to an 
unmanaged network 
This policy addresses a range of threats to, or related to, the use of IBVL data: 
 
Threat Description 
Loss Devices used to transfer, or transport work files could 
be lost or stolen 
Theft Sensitive corporate data is deliberately stolen and sold 
by an employee 
Copyright Software copied onto a mobile device could violate 
licensing 
Malware Virus, Trojans, Worms, Spyware and other threats could 
be introduced via a mobile device 
Compliance Loss or theft of financial and/or personal and 
confidential data could expose IBVL to the risk of non - 
compliance with various identity theft and privacy laws 
 
Addition of new hardware, software, and/or related components to provide additional mobile 
device connectivity will be managed at the sole discretion of IT. 
Non -sanctioned use of mobile devices to backup, store, and otherwise access any enterprise -related 
data is strictly forbidden. 
This policy is complementary to any other implemented policies dealing specifically with data 
access, data storage, data movement, and connectivity of mobile devices to any element of the IBVL 
network. Mobile Tablet Usage Policy

3. Audience 
This policy applies to all IBVL employees, including full and part -time staff, and the Board of 
Directors who utilize company -owned mobile devices to access, store, back up, relocate, or access 
any organization or member -specific data. 
Such access to this confidential data is a privilege, not a right, and forms the basis of the trust IBVL 
has built with its members, suppliers, and other constituents. 
Consequently, employment at IBVL does not automatically guarantee the initial and ongoing ability 
to use these devices to gain access to corporate networks and information.

4. Policy Detail 
The overriding goal of this policy is to protect the integrity of the private and confidential member 
and business data that resides within IBVL’s technology infrastructure. 
This policy intends to prevent this data from being deliberately or inadvertently stored insecurely on 
a mobile device or carried over an insecure network where it can potentially be accessed by 
unsanctioned resources. 
A breach of this type could result in loss of information, damage to critical applications, loss of 
revenue, and damage to IBVL’s public image. 
Therefore, all users employing a IBVL owned mobile device, connected to an unmanaged network 
outside of IBVL’s direct control, to backup, store, and otherwise access corporate data of any type 
must adhere to company -defined processes for doing so.

5. Affected Technology 
Connectivity of all mobile devices will be centrally managed by IBVL’s IT Department and will utilize 
authentication and strong encryption measures. 
To protect IBVL’s infrastructure, failure to adhere to these security protocols will result in immediate 
suspension of all network access privileges.

6. Responsibilities 
It is the responsibility of any employee or Board Member of IBVL, who uses a IBVL owned mobile 
device to access corporate resources, to ensure that all security protocols normally used in the 
management of data on conventional storage infrastructure are al so applied here. 
It is imperative that any IBVL owned mobile device that is used to conduct IBVL business be utilized 
appropriately, responsibly, and ethically. Failure to do so will result in immediate suspension of that 
user’s account. Based on this, the following rules must be observed: 
6.1 Access Control 
• IT reserves the right to refuse, by physical and non -physical means, the ability to connect 
mobile devices to IBVL and IBVL -connected infrastructure. IT will engage in such action if it 
feels such equipment is being used in such a way that puts IBVL’s syst ems, data, users, and 
members at risk. 
• Prior to initial use on the IBVL network or related infrastructure, all mobile devices must be 
registered with IT. IBVL will maintain a list of approved mobile devices and related software

applications and utilities, and it will be stored in the IT Document Storage location. Devices 
that are not on this list may not be connected to the IBVL infrastructure. To find out if a 
preferred device is on this list, an individual should contact the IB VL IT Department Service 
Desk. 
• Although IBVL IT/Admin currently allows only listed devices to be connected and carried in to 
the IBVL infrastructure, it reserves the right to update this list in the future after granting 
formal documented approval from client. 
• End users who wish to connect such devices to non -corporate network infrastructure to gain 
access to IBVL data must employ, for their devices and related infrastructure, a company -
approved personal firewall and any other security measure deemed necessary b y the IT 
Department. IBVL data is not to be accessed on any hardware that fails to meet IBVL’s 
established enterprise IT security standards. 
• All mobile devices attempting to connect to the IBVL network through an unmanaged network 
(i.e. the Internet) will be inspected using technology centrally managed by IBVL’s IT 
Department. Devices that are not corporate issued are not in compliance with IT’ s security 
policies and will not be allowed to connect except by provision of the Personal Device 
Acceptable Use and Security Policy. 
• IBVL owned laptop computers may only access the corporate network and data using a Secure 
Socket Layer (SSL) Virtual Private Network (VPN) or Internet Protocol Security (IPSec) VPN 
connection. The SSL or IPSec VPN portal Web address will be provided to use rs as required. 
• Smart mobile devices such as Smartphones, PDAs, and UMPCs will access the IBVL network 
and data using Mobile VPN software installed on the device by IT. 
6.2 Security 
• Employees using mobile devices and related software for network and data access will, 
without exception, use secure data management procedures. All mobile devices containing 
stored data owned by IBVL must use an approved method of encryption to protect dat a. 
Laptops must employ full drive encryption with an approved software encryption package. No 
IBVL data may exist on a laptop in clear text. All mobile devices must be protected by a strong 
password. Refer to the IBVL password policy for additional informa tion. Employees agree to 
never disclose their passwords to anyone, particularly to family members, if business work is 
conducted from home. 
• All users of corporate owned mobile devices must employ reasonable physical security 
measures. End users are expected to secure all such devices used for this activity whether 
they are actually in use and/or being carried. This includes, but is not limited to, passwords, 
encryption, and physical control of such devices whenever they contain IBVL data. Users with 
devices that are not issued by IBVL must adhere to the Personal Device Acceptable Use and 
Security Policy. 
• To ensure the security of IBVL equipment, mobile devices will be transported and stored as 
specified in the “Mobile Device Transport and Storage” procedure. 
• Passwords and confidential data should not be stored on unapproved or unauthorized non -
IBVL devices.

page_content:
• Any corporate owned mobile device that is being used to store IBVL data must adhere to the 
authentication requirements of IBVL’s IT Department. In addition, all hardware security 
configurations must be pre -approved by IBVL’s IT Department before any enterp rise data -
carrying device can be connected to it. 
• IT will manage security policies, network, application, and data access centrally using 
whatever technology solutions it deems suitable. Any attempt to contravene or bypass said 
security implementation will be deemed an intrusion attempt and will be dealt with in 
accordance with IBVL’s overarching security policy. 
• Employees, Board of Directors, and temporary staff will follow all enterprise -sanctioned data 
removal procedures to permanently erase company -specific data from such devices once their 
use is no longer required. For assistance with detailed data wipe proce dures for mobile 
devices, an individual should contact the IBVL IT Department Service Desk. This information is 
found in the IT Document Storage location. 
• In the event of a lost or stolen mobile device, it is incumbent on the user to report this to IT 
immediately. IBVL shall employ remote wipe technology to remotely disable and delete any 
data stored on a IBVL PDA or cell phone that is reported lost or stole n. If the device is 
recovered, it can be submitted to IT for re -provisioning. 
• Usage of location -based services and mobile check -in services, which leverage device GPS 
capabilities to share real -time user location with external parties, is prohibited within the 
workplace. This applies to both IBVL -owned and personal mobile devices be ing used within 
IBVL’s premises. 
• IT maintains the process for patching and updating mobile devices. A device’s 
firmware/operating system must be up -to-date in order to prevent vulnerabilities and make 
the device more stable. The patching and updating processes are the responsibility of IT for 
computing platforms (i.e. laptops). 
• IT maintains the process for security audits on mobile devices. Since handheld devices are not 
completely under the control of IBVL, a periodic audit will be performed to ensure the devices 
are not a potential threat to IBVL. 
6.3 Help and Support 
• IBVL’s IT Department will support its sanctioned hardware and software but is not accountable 
for conflicts or problems caused by the use of unsanctioned media, hardware, or software. 
This applies even to devices already known to the IT Department. 
• Employees, Board of Directors, and temporary staff will not make modifications of any kind to 
IBVL owned and installed hardware or software without the express approval of IBVL’s IT 
Department. This includes, but is not limited to, any reconfiguration of t he mobile device. 
• IT reserves the right, through policy enforcement and any other means it deems necessary, to 
limit the ability of end users to transfer data to and from specific resources on the IBVL 
network. 
6.4 Organizational Protocol 
• IT can and will establish audit trails and these will be accessed, published, and used without 
notice. Such trails will be able to track the attachment of an external device to a PC, and the 
resulting reports may be used for investigation of possible breac hes and/or misuse.

• To identify unusual usage patterns or other suspicious activity, the end user agrees to and 
accepts that his or her access and/or connection to IBVL’s networks may be monitored to 
record dates, times, duration of access, etc. This is done to identify accou nts/computers that 
may have been compromised by external parties. In all cases, data protection remains IBVL’s

• The end user agrees to immediately report, to his/her manager and IBVL’s IT Department, any

• IBVL will not reimburse employees if they choose to purchase their own mobile devices except

• IBVL prohibits the unsafe and unlawful use of mobile devices, including but not limited to,

with all state laws in which one is currently operating, regardin g same, hands -free 
requirements, etc. 
• Before being granted a device and access to IBVL resources, a mobile device user must 
understand and accept the terms and conditions of this policy.

Why Network Policy: - 
Network policies are implemented for several reasons, primarily to ensure security, manage resources 
effectively, and enforce compliance with organizational rules and regulations. Here are some key 
reasons why network policies are implemented:

1. Security: Network policies help in defining and enforcing security measures such as access 
control, encryption standards, and intrusion detection/prevention systems. By specifying who 
can access which resources and under what conditions, organizations can mitigate security 
risks and protect sensitive data.

metadata.section: Security: Network policies help in defining and enforcing security measures such

page_content:
2. Resource Management : Policies help in managing network resources efficiently. This includes 
prioritizing traffic, allocating bandwidth, and optimizing network performance. By setting 
policies, organizations can ensure that critical applications receive sufficient resources a nd 
that non -essential activities do not impact overall network performance.

metadata.section: Resource Management : Policies help in managing network resources efficiently. T

page_content:
3. Compliance: Many industries and organizations are subject to regulatory requirements and 
standards (such as GDPR, HIPAA, PCI -DSS). Network policies help ensure compliance with 
these regulations by defining how data is handled, transmitted, and stored within the 
netwo rk.

metadata.section: Compliance: Many industries and organizations are subject to regulatory requirem

page_content:
4. Operational Efficiency : Clear policies streamline network management and administration. 
They provide guidelines for troubleshooting, maintenance schedules, and user support, which 
ultimately contribute to smoother operations and reduced downtime.

metadata.section: Operational Efficiency : Clear policies streamline network management and admini

page_content:
5. Risk Management: By implementing policies that address risks associated with data breaches, 
unauthorized access, and other potential threats, organizations can proactively manage and 
mitigate risks to their network infrastructure.

page_content:
6. Legal and Liability Considerations : Network policies can also help in defining liability and 
responsibility in case of network breaches or incidents. They establish procedures for incident 
response, data recovery, and legal compliance in case of investigations or audits.

metadata.section: Legal and Liability Considerations : Network policies can also help in defining

page_content:
7. Employee Productivity: Policies can also impact employee productivity by defining acceptable 
use of company resources, internet access policies, and guidelines for remote work. Clear 
policies can prevent misuse of resources and ensure employees have appropriate access to 
necess ary tools and information. 
In summary, network policies are essential for maintaining the integrity, security, and efficiency of 
organizational networks. They provide guidelines and rules that govern how network resources are 
accessed, managed, and utilized, thereby supporting the o verall goals and objectives of the 
organization.

measures are implemented to protect confidentiality, i ntegrity, and availability of information and 
resources within the network. Here are key components typically included in a network security 
policy: Network Policy
