Server Security: - 
A server security policy specifically focuses on ensuring the security of servers within an organization's 
IT infrastructure. It outlines detailed guidelines, procedures, and best practices for securing servers 
to protect against unauthorized access, data breaches, and other security threats. Here are key 
components typically included in a server security policy:

metadata.section: Policy Review and Updates: Establishes a process for reviewing and updating the

page_content:
1. Server Classification and Inventory: 
o Defines categories of servers based on their criticality and sensitivity to data. 
o Establishes procedures for maintaining an up -to-date inventory of all servers, including 
their purpose, location, and responsible personnel.

2. Server Hardening: 
o Specifies baseline security configurations for servers, including operating system 
settings, services, and applications. 
o Defines procedures for implementing security hardening measures to minimize 
vulnerabilities, such as disabling unnecessary services, configuring firewall rules, and 
applying security patches promptly.

3. Access Control: 
o Defines policies and procedures for granting, modifying, and revoking access to servers 
based on the principle of least privilege. 
o Specifies authentication mechanisms (e.g., strong passwords, multi -factor 
authentication) and access control measures (e.g., role -based access control) to 
ensure only authorized personnel can access servers and sensitive data.

4. Network Security: 
o Specifies guidelines for securing server communication channels, including encryption 
protocols (e.g., TLS) for data in transit. 
o Defines firewall rules, network segmentation, and intrusion detection/prevention 
measures to protect servers from unauthorized network access and attacks.

5. Data Protection: 
o Outlines procedures for encrypting sensitive data stored on servers, both at rest and 
in transit. 
o Defines backup and recovery policies to ensure data availability and integrity in case 
of data loss or corruption.

6. Patch Management: 
o Establishes procedures for monitoring and applying security patches and updates to 
servers promptly. 
o Specifies testing and validation processes to ensure patches do not disrupt server 
operations or introduce new vulnerabilities.

7. Monitoring and Logging: 
o Defining requirements for monitoring server activities, including logging security 
events, access attempts, and system changes. 
o Specifies procedures for reviewing and analyzing logs regularly to detect suspicious 
activities, unauthorized access attempts, or security incidents.

8. Incident Response: 
o Outlines procedures for detecting, reporting, and responding to security incidents 
affecting servers. 
o Specifies roles and responsibilities of personnel involved in incident response, 
including containment, investigation, and recovery.

9. Physical Security: 
o Specifies physical security measures to protect server rooms or data centers from 
unauthorized access, theft, or environmental hazards. 
o Defines access controls, surveillance, and environmental monitoring practices to 
safeguard server hardware and infrastructure.

10. Compliance and Auditing: 
• Ensures server configurations and operations comply with relevant regulatory requirements, 
industry standards, and organizational policies. 
• Defines procedures for conducting regular audits or assessments of server security controls, 
configurations, and practices.

• Emphasizes the importance of security awareness and training programs for personnel 
responsible for managing and maintaining servers. 
• Specifies training requirements to educate personnel on server security policies, procedures, 
and best practices.

metadata.section: • Emphasizes the importance of security awareness and training programs for pers

page_content:
12. Policy Review and Updates: 
• Establishes a schedule for reviewing and updating the server security policy to address 
emerging threats, technological advancements, or organizational changes. 
• Defines a process for incorporating feedback from security assessments, incidents, or audits 
to continuously improve server security measures. 
Firewall, Router and Switches Policy: - 
A network devices policy specifically focuses on establishing guidelines, rules, and best practices for 
the configuration, management, and security of critical network devices such as firewalls, routers, 
and switches. These devices play crucial roles in ma naging network traffic, ensuring secure 
communications, and protecting against unauthorized access and cyber threats. Here are key 
components typically included in a network devices policy for firewalls, routers, and switches:

1. Purpose and Scope: 
• Defines the purpose of firewalls in the network infrastructure and specifies the scope of the 
policy, including which networks and devices are covered.

2. Configuration Standards: 
• Specifies baseline configurations for firewalls, including rulesets, access control lists (ACLs), 
and network zones. 
• Defining guidelines for configuring firewall policies based on security requirements, traffic 
filtering rules, and application -specific rules (e.g., for web servers, databases).

3. Access Control: 
• Establish rules and procedures for managing firewall access, including who can configure 
rules, review logs, and perform maintenance tasks. 
• Defines principles for least privilege and segmentation to restrict access based on roles and 
responsibilities.

4. Logging and Monitoring: 
• Outlines requirements for firewall logging, including types of events to log (e.g., denied 
packets, rule changes) and retention periods. 
• Specifies procedures for monitoring firewall logs, analyzing traffic patterns, and detecting 
anomalous behavior or security incidents.

5. Updates and Maintenance: 
• Defines procedures for applying firmware updates, security patches, and configuration 
changes to firewalls. 
• Specifies testing and validation processes to ensure updates do not disrupt firewall operations 
or introduce vulnerabilities.

6. Incident Response: 
• Outlines procedures for responding to security incidents involving firewalls, including 
detection, containment, investigation, and recovery.

• Specifies roles and responsibilities of personnel involved in incident response and 
communication protocols during incidents.

metadata.section: • Specifies roles and responsibilities of personnel involved in incident respons

page_content:
7. Compliance and Auditing: 
• Ensures firewall configurations comply with industry standards, regulatory requirements, and 
organizational policies (e.g., PCI DSS, HIPAA). 
• Defines procedures for conducting regular audits or assessments of firewall configurations, 
rulesets, and access controls.

1. Purpose and Scope: 
• Defines the role of routers in the network infrastructure (e.g., routing traffic, connecting 
network segments) and specifies policy coverage.

2. Configuration Standards: 
• Specifies baseline configurations for routers, including routing protocols, interface settings, 
and access control lists (ACLs). 
• Defines guidelines for securing router management interfaces (e.g., SSH, SNMP) and 
implementing authentication mechanisms.

3. Access Control: 
• Establishes procedures for managing router access, including user authentication, privilege 
levels, and remote access controls. 
• Defines restrictions on router configuration changes and procedures for auditing access logs.

4. Monitoring and Logging: 
• Outlines requirements for monitoring router performance, traffic patterns, and security 
events. 
• Specifies logging settings for router events, such as interface status changes, routing table 
updates, and security -related incidents.

5. Updates and Maintenance: 
• Define procedures for applying router firmware updates, security patches, and configuration 
changes. 
• Specifies backup and rollback procedures to recover from configuration errors or failures 
during updates.

6. Incident Response: 
• Outlines procedures for detecting and responding to router security incidents, including 
unauthorized access attempts or routing anomalies. 
• Specifies roles and responsibilities of personnel involved in incident response and 
communication protocols during incidents.

7. Compliance and Auditing: 
• Ensure router configurations comply with regulatory requirements, industry standards, and 
organizational policies. 
• Defines procedures for conducting regular audits or assessments of router configurations, 
security controls, and compliance with policies.

1. Purpose and Scope: 
• Defines the role of switches in the network infrastructure (e.g., local traffic switching, VLAN 
management) and specifies policy coverage.

2. Configuration Standards: 
• Specifies baseline configurations for switches, including VLAN configurations, port security 
settings, and spanning tree protocols. 
• Defines guidelines for securing switch management interfaces (e.g., SSH, SNMP) and 
implementing authentication mechanisms.

3. Access Control: 
• Establishes procedures for managing switch access, including user authentication, privilege 
levels, and remote access controls. 
• Defines restrictions on switch configuration changes and procedures for auditing access logs.

4. Monitoring and Logging: 
• Outlines requirements for monitoring switch performance, traffic patterns, and security 
events. 
• Specifies logging settings for switch events, such as port status changes, MAC address changes, 
and security -related incidents.

5. Updates and Maintenance: 
• Defining procedures for applying switch firmware updates, security patches, and configuration 
changes. 
• Specifies backup and rollback procedures to recover from configuration errors or failures 
during updates.

6. Incident Response: 
• Outlines procedures for detecting and responding to switch security incidents, including 
unauthorized access attempts or network anomalies. 
• Specifies roles and responsibilities of personnel involved in incident response and 
communication protocols during incidents.

7. Compliance and Auditing: 
• Ensures switch configurations comply with regulatory requirements, industry standards, and 
organizational policies. 
• Defines procedures for conducting regular audits or assessments of switch configurations, 
security controls, and compliance with policies. 
 
Implementation and Review: 
• Implementation Guidelines: Provides guidance on implementing the policies across different 
network segments, ensuring consistency and effectiveness. 
• Policy Review and Updates: Establishes a schedule for reviewing and updating network 
devices policies to address emerging threats, technological advancements, or organizational 
changes. 
• Training and Awareness: Emphasizes the importance of training and awareness programs to 
educate personnel on network devices policies, procedures, and best practices.

WiFi : - 
A Wi -Fi policy, also known as a wireless network policy or wireless security policy, outlines guidelines 
and rules for the secure deployment, management, and use of wireless networks within an 
organization. It ensures that Wi -Fi networks are properly confi gured and maintained to protect 
against unauthorized access, data breaches, and other security threats. Here are key components 
typically included in a Wi -Fi policy:

1. Purpose and Scope: 
• Purpose: Defines the objectives of the WiFi policy, emphasizing the importance of secure WiFi 
deployments in protecting organizational assets and data. 
• Scope: Specifies the coverage of the policy, including which locations, devices, and personnel 
are subject to its rules and guidelines.

2. WiFi Network Configuration: 
• Network Architecture: Describes the design and layout of the WiFi network, including access 
points (APs), SSID configurations, and network segmentation (if applicable). 
• Encryption Standards: Specifies encryption protocols (e.g., WPA2 -PSK, WPA3) required for 
securing wireless communications to prevent eavesdropping and data interception. 
• Authentication Methods: Defines methods for authenticating users and devices connecting 
to the WiFi network, such as pre -shared keys (PSK), 802.1X/EAP authentication, or captive 
portals.

3. Access Control: 
• User Access: Establishes policies for granting and revoking WiFi access permissions based on 
user roles, departments, or specific needs. 
• Guest Access: Defines procedures for providing temporary or restricted access to guests, 
contractors, or visitors, ensuring separation from internal network resources. 
• Device Management: Specifies guidelines for managing and securing devices (e.g., laptops, 
smartphones) connecting to the WiFi network, including BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) 
policies.

4. Security Measures: 
• Firewall and Intrusion Detection: Requires the implementation of firewalls and intrusion 
detection/prevention systems (IDS/IPS) to monitor and block unauthorized access attempts 
and malicious activities. 
• Endpoint Security: Mandates the use of up -to-date antivirus software, endpoint protection, 
and device encryption for devices connecting to the WiFi network. 
• Security Updates: Defines procedures for applying security patches and updates to WiFi 
infrastructure components (e.g., AP firmware, controllers) promptly to mitigate 
vulnerabilities.

5. Monitoring and Logging: 
• Network Monitoring: Outlines requirements for monitoring WiFi network traffic, 
performance metrics, and security events using logging and monitoring tools. 
• Event Logging: Specifies logging settings to record WiFi -related events, such as authentication 
attempts, connection failures, and security incidents, for auditing and analysis purposes.

• Acceptable Use: Establishes guidelines for acceptable use of the WiFi network, prohibiting 
activities that could compromise network security or violate organizational policies (e.g., 
illegal file sharing, accessing inappropriate content). 
• Bandwidth Management: Defines policies for managing and prioritizing bandwidth usage on 
the WiFi network to ensure fair access and optimal performance for business -critical 
applications.

metadata.section: • Acceptable Use: Establishes guidelines for acceptable use of the WiFi network,

page_content:
7. Incident Response: 
• Reporting Procedures: Outlines procedures for reporting WiFi network security incidents, 
suspicious activities, or breaches to the IT security team or designated personnel. 
• Response Plan: Defines steps for detecting, containing, investigating, and mitigating WiFi -
related security incidents, including communication protocols and escalation procedures.

8. Compliance and Legal Requirements: 
• Regulatory Compliance: Ensures that WiFi network deployments comply with relevant laws, 
regulations (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA), and industry standards (e.g., PCI DSS) regarding data 
protection and privacy. 
• Documentation and Audits: Defines requirements for documenting WiFi network 
configurations, security measures, and compliance with the WiFi policy, and conducting 
regular audits or assessments.

9. Training and Awareness: 
• User Education: Emphasizes the importance of security awareness training for employees, 
contractors, and guests regarding WiFi security best practices, risks, and responsibilities. 
• Policy Awareness: Ensures that personnel understand and comply with the WiFi policy, 
including procedures for reporting security concerns and seeking assistance.

10. Policy Review and Updates: 
• Review Schedule: Establishes a schedule for reviewing and updating the WiFi policy to address 
emerging threats, technological advancements, or organizational changes. 
• Feedback Mechanism: Defines a process for collecting feedback from stakeholders, 
conducting risk assessments, and incorporating lessons learned from WiFi security incidents. 
• Implementing a WiFi policy helps organizations maintain a secure and reliable wireless 
network infrastructure, safeguarding sensitive data and ensuring compliance with regulatory 
requirements. It provides clear guidelines for IT administrators, security pe rsonnel, and end -
users to follow, promoting a proactive approach to WiFi network security and risk 
management. 
 
Network Management and Access Mechanism: - 
A network management and access mechanism policy is essential for defining how network resources 
are managed, accessed, and secured within an organization. This policy ensures that network 
operations are efficient, secure, and compliant with organizational standards and regulatory 
requirements. Here are key components typically included in a network management and access 
mechanism policy:

• Purpose: Defines the objectives of network management and access mechanisms, 
emphasizing the importance of efficient operations and robust security. 
• Scope: Specifies which network resources, systems, and personnel are covered by the policy, 
including remote access considerations.

metadata.section: • Purpose: Defines the objectives of network management and access mechanisms,

page_content:
2. Network Management Practices: 
• Configuration Management: Establishes procedures for maintaining accurate and up -to-date 
network device configurations, including routers, switches, firewalls, and servers. 
• Change Management: Defines guidelines for implementing changes to network 
configurations, including testing, approval processes, and rollback procedures. 
• Asset Inventory: Requires maintaining an inventory of all network devices, including hardware 
specifications, software versions, and criticality assessments.

3. Access Control: 
• User Access: Specifies rules for granting, modifying, and revoking access to network resources 
based on user roles, responsibilities, and least privilege principles. 
• Authentication Mechanisms: Defines authentication methods (e.g., passwords, multi -factor 
authentication, certificates) required for accessing network devices and services. 
• Remote Access: Outlines procedures and security requirements for remote access to network 
resources, including VPN configurations, secure protocols (e.g., SSH, SSL/TLS), and monitoring 
of remote sessions.

4. Network Monitoring and Performance Management: 
• Monitoring Tools: Defines requirements for deploying network monitoring tools to monitor 
network performance, traffic patterns, and security events. 
• Performance Metrics: Establishes benchmarks and metrics for network performance (e.g., 
bandwidth utilization, latency, uptime) to ensure optimal operation and identify potential 
issues. 
• Capacity Planning: Includes procedures for forecasting network capacity requirements based 
on growth projections and business needs.

5. Security Measures: 
• Firewall and Intrusion Detection/Prevention: Specifies requirements for deploying firewalls 
and intrusion detection/prevention systems (IDS/IPS) to protect network boundaries and 
detect malicious activities. 
• Vulnerability Management: Defines procedures for identifying, assessing, and mitigating 
vulnerabilities in network devices and services through patch management and security 
updates. 
• Data Protection: Outlines encryption standards and procedures for securing sensitive data 
transmitted over the network, both internally and externally.

6. Incident Response: 
• Incident Reporting: Establishes procedures for reporting network security incidents, 
suspicious activities, or breaches promptly to the IT security team or designated personnel. 
• Response Plan: Defines roles, responsibilities, and steps for responding to network security 
incidents, including containment, investigation, and remediation efforts.

• Post -Incident Review: Includes processes for analyzing root causes of incidents, implementing 
corrective actions, and updating network management practices to prevent future 
occurrences.

metadata.section: • Post -Incident Review: Includes processes for analyzing root causes of inciden

page_content:
7. Compliance and Auditing: 
• Regulatory Compliance: Ensures network management practices comply with relevant laws, 
regulations (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA), and industry standards (e.g., PCI DSS) concerning data 
protection and privacy. 
• Audit Requirements: Defines procedures for conducting regular audits or assessments of 
network management practices, configurations, and access controls to verify compliance and 
identify gaps.

8. Documentation and Training: 
• Policy Documentation: Requires documenting network management policies, procedures, 
configurations, and access controls for reference and auditing purposes. 
• Training Programs: Emphasizes the importance of ongoing training and awareness programs 
for network administrators and users regarding network management best practices, security 
risks, and policy compliance.

9. Policy Review and Updates: 
• Review Schedule: Establishes a schedule for reviewing and updating the network 
management and access mechanism policy to address emerging threats, technological 
advancements, or organizational changes. 
• Feedback Mechanism: Defines a process for soliciting feedback from stakeholders, conducting 
risk assessments, and incorporating lessons learned from security incidents or audits. 
 
By implementing a comprehensive network management and access mechanism policy, organizations 
can ensure efficient network operations, mitigate security risks, and maintain compliance with 
regulatory requirements. This policy provides clear guidelines and standards for network 
administrators, IT personnel, and users to follow, promoting a secure and resilient network 
infrastructure.

A Patch Management Policy outlines the guidelines, responsibilities, and processes for managing and 
applying software patches to systems within an organization. The goal is to ensure that vulnerabilities 
are mitigated, systems are secure, and software is u p to date, reducing the risk of cyberattacks and 
improving the overall stability of IT infrastructure .

metadata.section: A Patch Management Policy outlines the guidelines, responsibilities, and process

page_content:
1. Purpose 
The purpose of the Patch Management Policy is to ensure that all systems, applications, and devices 
within the organization are regularly updated with the latest security patches, bug fixes, and 
performance improvements. This minimizes security risks and e nsures operational continuity.

2. Scope 
This policy applies to all hardware and software assets owned, operated, or managed by the 
organization, including: 
• Servers and workstations 
• Network devices (routers, firewalls, etc.) 
• Operating systems (Windows, macOS, Linux, etc.) 
• Applications (both third -party and internally developed) 
• Mobile devices 
• Virtualization platforms and containers

3. Responsibilities 
• IT/Systems Administrators: Responsible for identifying, testing, and applying patches in a 
timely manner. 
• Security Team: Collaborates with IT teams to prioritize patches based on security risks and 
vulnerability severity. 
• End Users: Must ensure their devices are configured to receive and install patches 
automatically where applicable. 
• Patch Management Team: Oversees the overall process, monitors patch deployment success, 
and ensures that documentation is maintained.

4. Patch Management Process 
4.1 Identification and Assessment 
• Regularly monitor vendor websites, mailing lists, security bulletins, and trusted sources for 
new patches or updates. 
• Use automated patch management tools to detect available patches for operating systems, 
applications, and hardware. 
• Evaluate the importance of each patch based on: 
o Criticality (security, functionality, or performance) 
o Risk posed by not applying the patch 
o Compatibility with current systems and applications Patch Management Policy

4.2 Testing 
• Test patches in a staging environment or on non -production systems to ensure they do not 
introduce new issues. 
• Use rollback strategies in case of failures or unforeseen consequences during the testing 
phase. 
• Maintain a documented record of all patches tested, including test results. 
4.3 Approval 
• Critical security patches should be approved for immediate deployment. 
• Non -critical patches should follow a more structured approval process, with timelines for 
deployment. 
4.4 Deployment 
• Deploy patches in a phased manner to minimize disruptions, starting with less critical systems 
and proceeding to more critical systems. 
• Ensure that backup procedures are in place before applying patches to systems. 
• If patches require a system reboot, ensure proper communication with stakeholders to plan 
for minimal downtime. 
4.5 Verification 
• After deployment, verify the successful installation of patches through automated scanning 
tools. 
• Review system logs for errors or unusual behavior following patch deployment. 
• Conduct regular vulnerability scans to ensure that all patches are applied and systems are 
secure. 
4.6 Documentation and Reporting 
• Maintain detailed records of patches applied, including the date, system impacted, version 
updated, and results of testing and deployment. 
• Generate reports on patch status for audits, compliance reviews, and internal assessments.

5. Patch Deployment Schedule 
• Critical Patches: Should be deployed within 24 -48 hours after release or evaluation. 
• Security Patches: Should be deployed within 1 week of release. 
• Non -Critical Patches: Should be deployed within a specified timeframe (e.g., 30 days). 
• Regular Updates: Patches should be reviewed monthly to ensure timely updates for non -
urgent issues.

6. Patch Rollback Procedures 
• Ensure that patches can be rolled back safely in the event of failure. 
• Maintain system backups to restore systems in case patch deployment causes instability or 
other issues. 
• Define clear steps for emergency patch removal or rollback in the event of failure.

• Ensure that patching practices align with industry standards, legal requirements, and 
regulatory compliance (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA). 
• Conduct periodic audits to assess patch compliance and identify any gaps in the patch 
management process.

metadata.section: • Ensure that patching practices align with industry standards, legal requiremen

page_content:
8. Exception Handling 
• Some patches may not be compatible with specific hardware or software configurations. 
• Exceptions must be documented and 
mitigation strategies applied where necessary. 
• An exceptional process should be in place to ensure any deferred patches are tracked and 
revisited at regular intervals.

9. Tools and Technologies 
• Utilize automated patch management software (e.g., WSUS, SCCM, SolarWinds, etc.) to 
streamline patch identification, testing, deployment, and reporting. 
• Ensure that tools are regularly updated and configured to minimize the risk of missed patches.

10. Training and Awareness 
• Provide training for IT personnel on the importance of patch management and proper 
deployment techniques. 
• Educate end users on the importance of keeping devices up to date and the potential risks of 
outdated software.

11. Monitoring and Auditing 
• Continuously monitor for new patches, vulnerabilities, and threats. 
• Perform regular audits to verify that patch management procedures are followed, and systems 
remain secure.

12. Policy Review and Updates 
• The Patch Management Policy should be reviewed annually to ensure it remains aligned with 
industry best practices and organizational needs. 
• Update the policy as required to address emerging security threats, changes in technology, or 
organizational changes. 
 
This policy ensures that patch management processes are consistent, efficient, and effective in 
maintaining system integrity and reducing security vulnerabilities.

1. Purpose 
The purpose of this Server Backup Policy is to establish procedures for safeguarding data, ensuring 
business continuity, and protecting against data loss due to system failures, cyber threats, or human 
errors.

2. Scope 
This policy applies to all servers managed by [Company Name], including on -premises and cloud -
based servers. It covers all critical data, applications, and configurations.

3. Backup Types & Frequency 
• Full Backup: Performed monthly to capture the entire system and data. 
• Incremental Backup: Performed daily to store changes made since the last backup. 
• Differential Backup: Performed mid -week to store changes made since the last full backup. 
• Real -time Replication: Implemented for mission -critical systems.

4. Backup Storage and Retention 
• Primary Storage: Backups will be stored on secure, high -capacity storage systems. 
• Offsite Storage: Encrypted copies will be maintained at a secure, geographically distant 
location.

5. Backup Security 
• Encryption: All backups will be encrypted using industry -standard encryption protocols. 
• Access Control: Only authorized personnel can access backup data. 
• Integrity Checks: Regular checksum validation to ensure backup integrity.

6. Backup Testing & Restoration 
• Backups will be tested at least once per quarter. 
• A detailed restoration process will be documented and followed. 
• Periodic disaster recovery drills will be conducted.

7. Responsibilities 
• IT Department: Ensures compliance with this policy, performs backups, and manages 
restoration. 
• Data Owners: Identify critical data and communicate retention requirements. 
• Security Team: Ensures that backup security measures are followed.

8. Compliance & Review 
• This policy will be reviewed annually and updated as needed. 
• Non -compliance may result in disciplinary actions. 
 Server Backup Policy

1. Objective 
To establish a consistent and comprehensive process for the periodic review of all servers (physical 
and virtual) to ensure optimal performance, security, compliance, and resource utilization.

2. Scope 
This policy applies to all servers managed by the IT infrastructure team, including production, 
development, test, and backup environments.

3. Review Frequency 
• Every 15 Days: All servers will undergo a basic health check to identify any early warnings or 
performance/security concerns. 
• Monthly: Performance monitoring, backup verification, and patch status checks. 
• Quarterly: Security audit, access review, and capacity planning. 
• Annually: Full configuration and compliance audit.

4. Review Parameters 
Each review must assess the following areas: 
• Performance Metrics (CPU, memory, disk I/O, network usage) 
• Security Logs & Events (intrusion attempts, unusual logins) 
• Patch and Update Status (OS and software updates) 
• User Access Controls (active users, admin rights, expired accounts) 
• Backup Status (success/failure logs, restoration tests) 
• Service/Process Health (critical services, resource hogs) 
• Hardware Utilization (for physical servers) 
• Antivirus/Endpoint Security status 
• Compliance Checks (as per ISO/ITIL/organization -specific standards)

5. Roles & Responsibilities 
• IT Infrastructure Team: Perform reviews, document findings, and implement 
recommendations. 
• Server Owners (Application/Project Teams): Coordinate with IT to validate server -specific 
requirements. 
• IT Manager: Review reports, escalate issues, and ensure policy adherence.

6. Documentation 
• Maintain a Server Review Checklist and Review Report for each server. 
• Store all reports in a centralized, access -controlled location. 
• Log identified issues, action plans, and resolution status in the ITSM tool or internal tracking 
system. 
 Server Review Policy

7. Compliance & Audit 
• All server reviews are subject to internal and external audits. 
• Non -compliance must be documented with a risk acceptance form or corrective action plan.

8. Exceptions 
Any deviation from this policy must be approved by the IT Manager and documented accordingly.

9. Review & Update 
This policy must be reviewed annually and updated as necessary to reflect changes in infrastructure, 
compliance requirements, or best practices.

10. Server Inventory Sheet 
A server inventory sheet will be maintained and updated periodically.
